observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. Facilitate the preparation of the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) together with supporting documents and undertake the necessary disposition of the unserviceable properties totaling P15,543,693.53 to avoid further deterioration and reduction of economic value, in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445; and b. Investigate the causes of the non-existing/missing PPEs totaling P6,886,269.96; and c. Identify the accountable officials for the non-existing/missing PPEs and require them to surrender the items. Otherwise, institute appropriate action/charges to persons/ officials accountable/responsible for the non-existing/ missing PPEs. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to provide disclosures on the non-existing/missing PPEs totaling P6,886,269.96 in the NTFS in accordance with Section 9.2(a) of COA Circular No. 2020-006.
observations.managementAction: The IIRUP for Motor Vehicles has been prepared, however, the IIRUP for Other PPE, Information and Communication Technology Equipment and Office Equipment was not yet submitted. The Management is still in the process of investigating the causes of missing PPEs. The Management has not yet identified the accountable officials for the non-existing/missing PPEs. The Management has no data regarding the missing properties. The General Services Office has been exhausting all the efforts to investigate the whereabouts of the missing properties, however, they couldn’t identify the accountable personnel for the missing items, while the Municipal Accountant agreed to comply with the recommendation in the ensuing year.
observations.reason: The IIRUP for Other PPE was not yet submitted due to lack of manpower in the General Services Office. There are no available records to determine the custody of the non-existing properties since there are no available records. No data regarding the missing properties was provided to the Municipal Accountant.

observations.reason: No data regarding the missing properties was provided to the Municipal Accountant. Required information in the Notes to Financial Statements was not yet provided in CY 2024 due to lack of manpower in the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Analyze the accounts payable outstanding for more than two years and revert to the Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund those that to be with no actual claims, administrative or judicial, or which are not covered with perfected contracts on record; and b. Conduct periodic review and monitoring of the Accounts Payable so the account will consist only of valid claims against the Municipal Government. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to verify the validity of payables aged more than two years and send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants or creditors.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant expressed his concurrence with the recommendation and indicated that review of the Accounts Payable is already ongoing. In addition, the Municipal Treasurer has already sent the confirmation letters to the concerned suppliers to confirm the validity of their claims. The sending of confirmation letters for Accounts Payable outstanding from CY 2022 to present will be facilitated in CY 2025.
observations.reason: The review of accounts takes longer than the usual due to lack of manpower. The unadjusted obligations from CY 2022 were not yet adjusted. The review of accounts payable for more than two years is ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal Engineer to ensure the conduct of detailed engineering and in-depth planning of proposed projects and identify the priority development projects that are procurement-and-implementation-ready; b. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to ensure optimal and timely utilization of 20 percent DF in the succeeding periods by including development projects that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready; and c. Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that all savings from completed PPAs and all unimplemented PPAs that are no longer feasible are considered for immediate realignment, subject to budgetary rules or reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of the 20 percent Development Fund for re-appropriation to other development projects.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer has re-assessed the viability of proposed projects and either implement those projects or remove and re-align unutilized funds for other projects. However, due to changes in priority projects in the implementation year, utilization was low in CY 2024. The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator is committed to ensure optimal and timely utilization of 20 percent DF in the succeeding periods. The Municipal Budget Officer indicated that PPAs to be funded in the continuing appropriation are not yet finalized.
observations.reason: Problems were encountered in the implementation schedule due to changes in PPAs and issues in procurement (e.g., deed of lot donation for project site). The utilization rate was only 24.96% in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council through the MDRRMO to prepare and submit the required comprehensive LDRRMF Investment Plan specifying detailed list of projects and activities funded out of the previous years’ unexpended LDRRMF to ensure optimal utilization and monitoring.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO commented that the LDRRMF Investment Plan and Annual Investment Plan were formulated prior to his assumption as MDRRMO, but agreed that they will include the PPAs under Special Trust Fund, including the specific funding source in the next planning period. The MDRRMO has coordinated with the Accounting Office regarding the utilization of the Special Trust Fund, however adjustments in the Special Trust Fund were not considered in the preparation of LDRRMF Investment Plan.
observations.reason: The MDRRMO overlooked the inclusion of PPAs under Special Trust Fund; LDRRMFIP was formulated prior to the MDRRMO assumption and was not updated to reflect special trust fund balances.

observations.recommendation:
1.5. We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to prepare
adjusting journal entries and record in the books the reconciling items
affecting the book balance as identified in the Bank Reconciliation Statements.

2.23. We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Inventory Committee to:
❖ Conduct a complete physical inventory of all properties owned by the
City Government and render the duly signed report to the Audit Team
not later than January 31 of the ensuing year using the prescribed
format;
❖ Reconcile and investigate the noted deficiencies and prepare a
reconciliation report;
❖ Identify all unserviceable properties and undertake the necessary
procedures to dispose them; and
❖ Closely monitor the implementation and facilitate the completion of the
one-time cleansing of the PPE account balances to arrive at an accurate
and reliable amount of PPE account.
b. Direct the City Accountant to:
❖ Maintain the proper PPELC for each PPE account;
❖ Work-back to identify the composition of the lump sum amounts; and
❖ Reconcile the result of the physical count with the PPELC and effect
the necessary corrections, if any.
c. Instruct the City GSO to:
❖ Maintain an updated PC for each category;
❖ Reconcile the result of the physical count with the PCs and effect the
necessary corrections, if any; and
❖ Initiate the disposal of unserviceable items identified during physical
inventory.

3.8. We recommended that Management:
a. Cause the formal transfer of the said motor vehicles to the donee-
barangays by executing the Deed of Donation and other supporting
documents necessary to effect donation; and
b. Instruct the City Accountant to record the necessary accounting entries to
derecognize the transferred assets once the transfer has been officially
completed.

4.13 We recommended that Management:
a. Adhere to the general process of handling and accounting for
inventories and supplies and materials as prescribed by the MNGAS
for LGUs;
b. Advise the City Accountant to submit the SSMI, RIS and Relief
Distribution Sheet to support the JEV recognizing the supplies and
materials expenses totaling ₱72,470,683.51.
c. Advise the GSO to maintain stock cards and record deliveries and
issuances therein;
d. Advise the GSO to document each issuance of inventory using the RIS
form;
e. Advise the implementing departments such as the GSO, CSWDO,
CHO, CVO to document each issuance of inventory/supplies, prepare
the SSMI and forward the same to the Accounting Unit to serve as basis
in the preparation of JEV and recording of issuances;
f. Advise the Department Heads to furnish the City Accounting Office a
copy of the JEV for supplies received from other government agencies;
g. Advise the Accounting Unit to account for the inventories using the
perpetual inventory method; and
h. Instruct the Inventory Committee to regularly conduct an annual
physical inventory count and reconcile the same with the records of the
accounting and the GSO.

5.6 We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to:
a. Ensure that proper documentation and evidence be maintained to
substantiate the derecognition of these breeding stocks;
b. Derecognize the breeding stocks from the financial records; and
c. Conduct a regular physical verification of assets, particularly breeding
stocks, to ensure their existence and condition.

6.7 We recommended that Management advise the City Accountant to exhaust all
possible means to locate the records of the dormant receivables in order to
substantiate the balance and effect collection. In case all efforts fail,
Management may require the City Accountant to request the write-off of the
dormant accounts, subject to the requirements and procedures for proper
disposition and derecognition of dormant accounts.

7.7 We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to provide
adequate disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements affecting the loans
payable of the City Government such as the purpose, term, maturity date,
interest rate and conditions and restrictions thereto.

8.7 We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to gather the
original documents and validate the Other Payables account that have been
outstanding for more than two years. If found invalid or no original
supporting documents can be found, the City may revert said payables to the
unappropriated surplus.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Recompute all the depreciation expenses incurred and presented in the PPE lapsing schedule for current and prior years to ensure the accuracy and valuation of PPE and its corresponding accumulated depreciation accounts in the Financial Statements and make the necessary adjustments; and

b. Ensure that depreciation expenses are properly calculated in compliance with Philippine Application Guidance Nos. 3 and 4 of IPSAS 17.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to use the OMOE account only for expenditures that cannot be classified under specific expense accounts, in accordance with IPSAS 1 and COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to disclose the required supporting information in the Notes to Financial Statements in accordance with IPSAS for the ensuing year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to follow the procedures in the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs under Sections 7 and 8 of COA Circular No. 2020-006; and

b. Municipal Accountant to provide proper disclosures on the non-existing/missing PPEs, in accordance with Section 9.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-006, to provide additional information in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to review and adjust the books of accounts by drawing a Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify the misclassified accounts, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Analyze the Accounts Payable outstanding for more than two years and revert to the Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund those that to be with no actual claims, administrative or judicial, or which are not covered with perfected contracts on record; and

b. Conduct periodic review and monitoring of the Accounts Payable so the account will consist only of valid claims against the Municipal Government.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to verify the validity of payables aged more than two years and send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants or creditors.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal General Services Officer to prepare and issue the ICS for semi-expendable property totaling P578,300.00, and to likewise prepare and maintain the SPC, RegSPI, and RSPI, for the establishment of proper control and accountability over the semi-expendable property; and

b. Municipal Accountant to maintain the SPLC to record the acquisition, description, custody, impairment, issue/transfer/disposal, repair history, and other information about the property.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal General Services Officer to prepare the corresponding IIRUP for PPEs totaling P570,780.00 by filling-out the inventory portion and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor and to the Appraisal and Disposal Committee to initiate the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties, in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and the rules and procedures provided in COA Circular No. 92-386;

b. Appraisal and Disposal Committee to conduct an appraisal of the unserviceable properties and submit the appraisal report to the Office of the Auditor; and

c. Appraisal and Disposal Committee to facilitate the disposal of the Unserviceable Properties promptly through sale or negotiation to prevent further deterioration and loss of additional income.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General Services Officer/BAC Secretariat to provide the lacking information to the noted 370 POs and establish control and/or monitoring system to ensure that all contracts/ POs, complete with necessary details are submitted to the Office of the Auditor within five working days after their execution/issuance, in compliance with Sections 3.1.1 and 3.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and Item B of COA Circular No. 96-010.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer to prepare a contract, MOA, or JO between the Municipality of Alaminos and each JO worker to establish a clear and binding agreement and ensure that comprehensive provisions, including but not limited to the workers’ duties, responsibilities, terms of employment, payment structure, and the agreed method of payment (daily wage or piece of work) were stated in the contract to facilitate the proper validation of workers performance.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Development Council, through the MPDC and in coordination with the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Budget Officer to:

a. Ensure optimal and timely utilization of 20 percent DF in the succeeding periods by including development projects that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready according to DBM, DOF and DILG JMC No. 2020-01;

b. Conduct detailed engineering and in-depth planning for each project to ensure that the priority development projects are feasible for implementation; and

c. Identify the specific PPAs to be funded from the continuing appropriations of 20 percent DF to ensure the optimal utilization of the fund.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council through the MDRRMO to include and present under a separate caption the list of specific PPAs to be funded from the previous years’ unexpended LDRRMF, indicating therein the specific funding source of the year of transfer of each PPA in the LDRRMFIP, in accordance with Item in 5.1.2 and Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to prepare an adjusting journal entry to reclassify those short-term highly liquid investments from the investment account to the cash account for the fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Adjustment was presented in the Statement of Financial Position as of March 31, 2024, TD Placements of the City with LBP and DBP renewable every 30 days and 90 days, respectively, were reclassified from Investments to Cash and Cash Equivalents.
observations.reason: The City Accountant had prepared an adjusting journal entry to reclassify those short-term highly liquid investments from the investment account to the cash account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to prepare an adjusting journal entry to reclassify those short-term highly liquid investments from the investment account to the cash account for the fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management advise the City Accountant to exhaust all possible means to locate the records of the dormant receivables in order to substantiate the balance and effect collection. In case all efforts fail, Management may require the City Accountant to request the write-off of the dormant accounts, subject to the requirements and procedures for proper disposition and derecognition of dormant accounts.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Engineer to exhaust all possible means to validate the existence and status of the said projects and submit a copy of the report to the Audit Team and Office of the City Accountant to serve as basis in preparing the adjusting journal entry
We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to adjust the carrying value of the intangible assets to reflect their carrying value.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant:
To gather the original documents and validate the accounts payable that have been outstanding for more than two years. If found invalid or no original supporting documents can be found, the City may revert said payables to the unappropriated surplus; and
To ensure that all supporting documents are complete before preparing the JEV to record the transactions and attached the original supporting documents to the JEV for submission to the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to:
Coordinate with the DA-RFU I on the status of the soft loan program and finalize the settlement of the said loan;
Exert effort to review/retrieve record of payments/settlements, if any; and
Prepare and maintain subsidiary ledger and other relevant records or documents on all payable accounts.

We further recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to record the income and expenses of the ACDC instead of recording under the Due to GOCCs account for fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of charging expenditures against the LDRRMF that are not directly supportive of the implementation of disaster risk reduction and management programs and activities. Instead, these expenditures should be charged against the budget of the CDRRMO under the General Fund.

Direct the City Accountant to use accrual basis of accounting in recognizing the income earned from the ACDC by timely recording in the books all outstanding PhilHealth claims;
Direct the City Accountant to effect the necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct income and outstanding PhilHealth claims of the ACDC; and
Direct the Medical Records Officer of ACDC to regularly provide the City Accountant a copy of the list of outstanding PhilHealth claims for proper recording.

We recommended that the City Treasurer continue to send notices to delinquent taxpayers and conducting informational campaigns especially on the granting of discounts. This is aimed at encouraging prompt payment among taxpayers, thereby generating more revenue that can be appropriated by the City Government for the implementation of additional programs and projects benefiting the constituents.

require the business establishments paying business taxes to submit a copy of audited financial statements and/or ITR pursuant to the provisions of the City Revenue Code of the LGU in order to serve as basis in the computation of business tax.
Instruct the City Treasurer to cause the collection of the deficient business taxes totaling ₱9,565,185.27 from the suppliers concerned; and
Require the City Treasurer to be judicious in assessing the business taxes by considering all data available to ensure the correct assessment.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
• draw Journal Entry Voucher to drop the noted small tangible items from the
books of accounts by debiting Prior Year’s Adjustment and the affected
Accumulated Depreciation accounts, and by crediting the affected PPE
accounts in the amounts of P799,837.22, P1,031,288.80 and P1,831,126.02,
respectively for General Fund books and P401,177.94, P637,447.06 and
P1,038,625.00, respectively for Special Education Fund books; and
• record subsequent purchases of small tangible items as Inventories and
recognize as outright expense upon issuance to end-users, in accordance
with Section 4.2(e) of COA Circular No. 2020-006.

b. Municipal General Services Officer to:
• exclude in the property records the noted small tangible items with a total
cost of P2,869,751.02 by canceling the issued PAR and replacing it with ICS;
and
• ensure that subsequent issuances of small tangible items are supported with
ICS for monitoring and control purposes, in accordance with Section 2.3 of
COA Circular No. 2005-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
• revert the 99 Accounts Payable totaling P1,241,011.23 proven to be without
valid contracts and/no actual claims to the unappropriated surplus of the
General Fund;
• ensure that all past due and unpaid obligations are covered by perfected
contracts duly supported with complete documentation, in compliance with
Section 98 of PD No. 1445; and
• recognize liability only when the goods and services are accepted or
rendered and supplier/creditor bills are received, in compliance with Section
4(s) of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

b. Municipal Budget Officer to obligate only the actual amount of obligation based
on the approved Purchase Order or contract and not on the allotment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
• draw Journal Entry Voucher for the cancellation of the 32 stale checks by
debiting Cash in Bank – LCCA account and crediting the affected liability
accounts by P496,866.29, P392,843,90 and P44,860.72 for General Fund
books, Special Education Fund books and Trust Fund books, respectively;
and
• ensure that stale checks are immediately adjusted in the books of accounts
to reflect the accurate balance of Cash in Bank account at any given period,
in compliance with Section 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

b. Municipal Treasurer to send written notice to the concerned payee on the
existence of the check at least one month before it becomes stale.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. draw necessary adjustment in the books of accounts to correct the erroneous
entry made in the recoupment of the mobilization fee/advance payment
amounting to P131,061.26;
b. pay Lake City Builders the underpayment of P9,000.00 due to the erroneous
computation of the remaining mobilization fees/advance payment to be
recouped from the final progress payment; and
c. ensure that mobilization fees/advance payments to contractors are properly
recouped from the progress payments to avoid overpayment/underpayment to
contractors and misstatement of accounts, in compliance with Items 4.3 and 5.3
of the R-IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General
Services Officer to:

a. complete the lacking information from the noted 344 POs to avoid the
issuance of Notice of Suspension; and
b. adhere with Item B of COA Circular No. 96-010 on the preparation of PO
complete with all the necessary information pertaining to the procurement
to ensure that the supplier will deliver the items in accordance with the terms
and conditions of the contract.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all the Offices concerned
to stop the practice of preparing their own POs instead of the Municipal General
Services Office, in accordance with the provisions of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume
II.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Officer to submit a written justification as to the
reason of using the noted financial assistance for other purpose.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that trust funds are to
be spent only for the purpose for which it was created, in accordance with
Section 4(3) of PD No. 1445.

b. Non-reversion of unutilized balance totaling P99,098.00 to the National Treasury

Item 3.7 of DBM LBC No. 130 provides that “The financial assistance to LGUs,
charged against the NDRRM Fund, may be utilized by the beneficiary LGUs until
December 31, 2021. Funds which remain unutilized after December 31, 2021 shall be
reverted to the National Treasury by the recipient LGUs.”

As of December 31, 2021, the unutilized balance of the Financial Assistance to LGUs
charged against the NDRRM Fund amounted to P99,098.00, computed as follows:
Unprogrammed Fund P 93.00
Unutilized balance of PPA: Planting materials to affected 27,000.00
farmers due to typhoons and calamities
Unutilized balance of PPA: Food assistance to affected 72,005.00
families due to typhoons, disasters and calamities
Total P99,098.00

Verification of records disclosed that the above unutilized balance was not reverted to
the National Treasury.

Return of any unutilized amount of trust fund is required for the funds to be liquidated.
The non-reversion of unused funds resulted in the unnecessary and prolonged
recognition of the liability and deprived the national government of funds that could
have been used to finance other beneficial projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Treasurer to return the unutilized balance of financial assistance
totaling P99,098.00 to the National Treasury, in accordance with Item 3.7 of
DBM LBC No. 130; and
b. Municipal Accountant to submit to the Office of the Auditor copy of the
updated liquidation report and other supporting documents relative to the
return of unutilized balance to National Treasury.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. submit the required lacking documents complete or supplied with necessary
information needed to support the noted emergency procurement to avoid the
issuance of Notice of Suspension; and
b. ensure that all procurements undertaken through Negotiated Procurement –
Emergency Cases are supported with complete documentation, in compliance with
Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Item 4 of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator to:

a. effectively plan the priority development PPAs to be included in the AIP by
considering its feasibility and necessity and strictly follow the implementation
timeframe for monitoring purposes; and
b. optimize the utilization of the 20 per cent Development Fund to help achieve
desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the
Municipality, in accordance with Item 5 of DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to address the
discrepancies noted on the reported unexpended balance of LDRRMF between their
records and make the necessary adjustments thereto.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Officer to regularly prepare and submit the monthly
Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF, duly certified by the Municipal
Accountant, to the Office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of
each month, in compliance with Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that Management require the City Agriculture Office and other offices concerned to coordinate with and submit to the City GSO the supporting documents on the issuances of the inventories concerned which shall serve as basis in preparing the RIS and SSMI and for the GSO to submit the said reports to the City Accountant.

We also recommended that Management require the City Accountant to record the issuances of said inventories based on the SSMI to be provided by the City GSO in order to present the accurate balance of Inventory account in the financial statements.

We further recommended that Management create an Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of inventories semestrally, to reconcile the results thereof with the records of the City GSO and City Accountant and to submit a copy of the RPCI to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year.

We recommended that Management:
Require the City Cooperatives Officer to undertake the transfer of the titles of the breeding stocks in the name of the City Government and to provide copy thereof to the City GSO and City Accountant;
Require the City Cooperatives Officer in coordination with the City Accountant and Sangguniang Panlungsod to submit a copy of duly signed and notarized MOA and SP Resolution authorizing the City Mayor to enter into a MOA; and
Require the City Accountant to draw the necessary Journal Entry Voucher to derecognize the Breeding Stock from the books of accounts of the City Government once the Deed of Donation in each barangay is approved.

We recommended that Management:
Require the City Accountant to adjust the Current Appropriation for PS, MOOE and CO in the total amount of ₱25,190,264.00 presented in the Final Budget in the SCBAA consistent with the changes in the original budget through the seven supplemental budgets;
Direct the City Accountant to analyze the transactions affecting the balance of Continuing Appropriation and to make the necessary adjustments on the discrepancy between the ending balance in CY 2020 and forwarded balance in CY 2021 in the amount of ₱772,985.37; and
Require the City Budget Officer in coordination with the City Accountant to revert the unexpended balances of appropriations for MOOE to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund at the end of the fiscal year.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to send demand letters to the accountable officers concerned to settle their unliquidated cash advances and prepare the request for authority to write-off for the non-moving and long outstanding cash advances in accordance with the procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to strictly observe compliance with the provision of the MNGAS for LGUs by recording purchases of supplies and materials to the inventory account using the perpetual inventory method to present the accurate balance of the Inventory account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to retrieve the documents and details of the receivables totaling ₱1,360,639.40 in order to determine the name of the borrowers/debtors to whom demand letters will be issued to enforce collection thereof. In case of non-collection, instruct the City Accountant to request for authority to write-off the dormant receivable accounts consistent with the requirements and procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that Management require the City Assessor to facilitate the transfer and registration of ownership with the LRA on the remaining 41 parcels of land in accordance with Section 39 (2) of PD No. 1445 to establish absolute ownership and protect the interest of the City Government.

We also recommended that Management require the City Accountant to verify the Deeds of Donation and other supporting documents on 23 parcels of land donated to DepEd and if found in order, drop the corresponding costs from the books of accounts of the City Government to reflect the correct balance of the land account.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to verify the Accounts Payable account and submit copies of perfected contracts and documents showing that the goods/services were actually delivered and received but not yet paid to establish the validity of the accounts payable account amounting to ₱4,035,708.63; otherwise, revert the said amount to the Government Equity of the General and Special Education Funds.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant, in coordination with the City Treasurer, to exert effort to obtain the records on the shares of barangays from RPT collections in the previous years totaling ₱1,826,603.94 in order to identify the barangays and the corresponding amount due them and subsequently, cause the remittance/release of the shares immediately.

We also recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant, in coordination with the City Treasurer, to release the RPT shares of the barangays within five days after the end of each quarter to enable the barangays to have additional funds for their operations.

We recommended that Management require the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to process the requests for monetization of vacation leave credits for a minimum of 10 days and maximum of 30 days in a given year and stop the practice of granting the requests for monetization below the required minimum of 10 days and of sick leave credits except on justifiable reasons.

We recommended that Management reduce the allowance of the BNS in accordance with the maximum amount provided in Section 6 of PD No. 1569 to avoid incurrence of additional maintenance and other operating expenses.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant, in coordination with the City Human Resources Officer, to ensure that the practice of granting benefits such as per diems and attendance to trainings and seminars to Job Order personnel be discontinued and to strictly adhere to the provisions provided in the CSC Memorandum Circular.

We recommended that Management require the City Human Resources Officer (HRMO) to review the current roster of consultants and assess the need to engage their services and consider to hire their services only when the relevant experience and level of expertise or attention required for the government project or related activities are not available in the agency and are not redundant to the functions of the existing offices or regular employees of the City Government to avoid incurrence of unnecessary expenditures.

We recommended that Management submit specific statutory authority authorizing the use of the AICS fund for the payment of livelihood assistance.

We further recommended that Management be more cautious in granting financial assistance to ensure that the assistance are only granted to the qualified recipients.

We recommended that Management submit specific statutory authority for the payment of financial assistance to Job Orders and Contract of Service workers of the LGU and various personnel of the national government agency, non-governmental organizations and barangays as well as the criteria used in selecting the beneficiaries and valid IDs of the recipients for further evaluation of the Audit Team.

We also recommended that Management be more cautious in granting financial assistance to ensure that these are only granted or given to the qualified recipients.

We recommended that Management require the City Budget Officer and the Local Finance Committee to:
refrain from using the appropriations for PS, MOOE and CO to augment the appropriation of any expense item belonging to another expense class;
avoid the reversion of appropriations unless it pertains to reversion of unexpended balances of appropriations to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund at the end of the fiscal year and the reversion of continuing appropriations unless obligations therefor have been fully paid or otherwise settled; and
prepare the Supplemental Budget in accordance with the requirements of Section 321 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that Management grant honoraria to the BAC Members, BAC-TWG and BAC Secretariat within the limitation set forth under Section 11 of RA No. 9184 and its 2016 IRR and to require the City Accountant in coordination with the BAC Secretariat, to submit the lacking documents for further review and evaluation of the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management:
Require the City Accountant and the Head of the BAC Secretariat to submit the lacking documents for further evaluation of the Audit Team;
Require the BAC and the TWG to ensure that all required documents are submitted by the bidders and duly evaluated by the TWG and the BAC.
Direct the BAC to require the posting of performance security prior to the signing of the contract to guarantee the faithful performance by the winning bidder of its obligations under the contract.

We recommended that Management require the Chairman of the BAC with the assistance of the BAC Secretariat, to submit immediately the contract and supporting documents of one project with total contract cost of ₱2,369,392.94 to the Audit Team and to observe the prescribed period of submission of five working days from the execution of a contract to facilitate the review and evaluation thereof.

We recommended that Management ensure collection of the delinquent accounts on real property taxes and stall rentals through the City Treasurer by adopting the procedures and remedies provided in Sections 254 and 256 of RA No. 7160, to provide additional funds to support the operations and programs of the City Government geared towards the welfare of the constituents.

We also recommended that Management include the payment of delinquent accounts for stall rentals in the policies and procedures of the City Government before approving the renewal of Business Permits and Licenses of stall owners to intensify the collection efforts on delinquent stall rentals.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to identify the specific gender issues in the GAD Plan and Budget caused by the unequal situation between women and men in the locality and to formulate PPAs that are responsive to these gender issues in order to attain the main intentions in promoting gender-responsive governance, protection and fulfilment of women’s human rights and the promotion of women’s economic empowerment.

We further recommended that Management, through the GFPS, prioritize in the succeeding GAD Plan and Budget the recurring/emerging gender issues in the City, results of gender assessment of the organization or its PPAs and other GAD PPAs identified in the GAD Code of the LGU that have not been considered in the previous year to achieve a more effective GAD Plan and Budget.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Honorable City Mayor instruct the City GSO to:
a. Cause the preparation of the updated Inventory and IIRUP together with the corresponding appraisal assessment, Appraisal Committee evaluation and appraisal report;
b. Facilitate the submission of request for disposal of the aforementioned properties to the Office of the Audit Team for inspection, evaluation and review by the COA Technical Services Office; and
c. Ensure to undertake immediate disposal of the subject properties upon receipt of the result of inspection from COA, so as to prevent further deterioration, obsolescence and decline of value and in order to generate additional funds from sale thereof.
observations.managementAction: Management prepared and submitted the IIRUP together with the corresponding appraisal report of the Appraisal Committee of the City Government; requested the disposal of the unserviceable properties; and scheduled the public auction of the aforementioned properties.
observations.reason: The Audit Team verified the submission of the IIRUP by the City Government. Committed and appraisal report of the Appraisal Committee of the City Government. Implemented The Audit Team verified the submission of the request for disposal of the unserviceable properties and forwarded the same to the COA Regional Technical Evaluation Services (RTES) for inspection and appraisal. Implemented The City GSO already scheduled the public auction of the unserviceable properties on May 21, 2021 based on the appraisal report of the COA RTES.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Honorable City Mayor instruct the City GSO to install markings “For Official Use Only” with the official logo of the City, and to secure government plates on the motor particularly those newly acquired motor vehicles of the LGU in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61 to ensure the effective conservation of energy and proper utilization of government motor vehicles.
observations.managementAction: Management installed markings “For Official Use Only” with the official logo of the City Government.
observations.reason: The Audit Team verified the installation of the markings “For Official Use Only” with the official logo of the City Government.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit specific statutory authority for the payment of financial assistance to JOs and COS workers of the LGU and various personnel of the national government agency, non-governmental organizations and barangays and the criteria used in selecting the beneficiaries pursuant to Section 4(1) and 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 for further evaluation of the Audit Team; otherwise, disbursements pertaining thereto shall be suspended or disallowed in audit. We further recommended that Management be more cautious in granting only financial assistance to qualified recipients.
observations.managementAction: Management deducted the ₱2,000.00 financial assistance to JOs and COS workers to the ₱3,000.00 gratuity pay granted to contractual and job order workers in the government as per AO No. 20 dated January 10, 2020. Management ensured that only qualified recipients were granted financial assistance.
observations.reason: The Audit Team verified that the City Government deducted the ₱2,000.00 financial assistance to contractual and job order workers. The Audit Team verified that only qualified recipients were granted the financial assistance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management thru the initiative of the Honorable City Mayor and enactment of SP Resolution, cause the transfer of the costs or book value of the PPE to the end users or recipient schools and the barangays to conform with the provisions of paragraph 14 of IPSAS 17 - PPE. The transfer of properties should be supported with complete documents to ensure proper recording in the books of the LGU and of the recipient agencies. We also recommended that the City Accountant provide the required depreciation costs of the properties in the books of accounts pending the transfer of ownership to the end users to present the actual valuation of the properties in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The SP approved Resolution No. 2020-196 dated November 10, 2020 authorizing the City Mayor to execute a deed of donation for the said implemented buildings. The City Accountant provided the required depreciation costs of the said properties in the books of accounts pending transfer of ownership to the end users.
observations.reason: The Audit Team verified the SP Resolution and noted that the transfer of the implemented buildings and the recording of depreciation costs were properly provided and recorded in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Honorable City Mayor, through the City Planning and Development Officer, and the City Budget Officer, ensure that all PPAs charged against the 20% Development Fund are in consonance with the provisions of Sections 2.0 and 3.0 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 and implemented within the target dates to attain the intended purpose of the fund; and to require the City Engineer to provide documents to support any delay in the implementation of projects; otherwise, impose liquidated damages pursuant to Annex E, Item 8 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184. We also recommended that Management carry-out the aforementioned expenses in the General Fund and Special Education Fund in accordance with its Appropriation Ordinance.
observations.managementAction: The City Government has already completed the said PPAs charged against the 20% DF. The City Engineer submitted copies of Certificate of Completion and documents to support the delay.
observations.reason: The Audit Team verified the submitted copies of certificate of completion and noted that the projects were 100% completed as of December 31, 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure compliance with Section 4, paragraph 2 of PD No. 1445 by executing a Contract of Lease / Agreement with the CHIEMPCO which shall serve as the basis on the imposition and collection of rental fees and utility bills to enable the LGU to generate additional funds and also to protect the interest of the City.
observations.managementAction: Management submitted the request for allocation of an office space by the Cooperative through Board Resolution No. 21-S-2008 dated December 8, 2008. Management informed that one of the privileges by cooperatives organized among government employees is the free use of any available space in their agency.
observations.reason: The Audit Team reviewed the document and the quoted provision of RA No. 9520.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure compliance with Section 322 of RA No. 7160 and Section 5.1.11 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 on the treatment of unexpended / unobligated balance of LDRRMF for Capital Outlay by requiring the reversion of the Capital Outlay in the amount of ₱6,981,436.70 back to the General Fund to ensure its utilization to its intended purpose and to present fairly the balance of the Trust Liabilities – DRRM account in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant informed that due to COVID-19 pandemic and the declaration of ECQ in the Province of Pangasinan, the 2019 Special Trust Fund was exhausted for COVID-19 relief operations of the City Government.
observations.reason: The Audit Team verified the SP Resolution and noted that the 2019 Special Trust Fund was used for COVID-19 relief operations conducted by the City Government during the ECQ in the Province of Pangasinan.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict compliance with the procurement provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and the fundamental principle on financial transactions prescribed in Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and to:
a. Require the Bids and Awards Committee to submit explanation on how the prices quoted by the Suppliers in the four (4) procurement of meals and snacks were exactly the same with the estimated unit costs of all the items in the Purchase Request of the LGU; and
b. Instruct the BAC Secretariat and the City Accountant to submit the lacking supporting documents immediately to support the propriety, legality and validity of the paid disbursements.
observations.managementAction: The BAC submitted justification on the said procurement of meals and snacks and submitted the lacking supporting documents to support the disbursement of funds.
observations.reason: The Audit Team validated the submitted justification / explanation and the supporting documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure compliance
with the procedures provided by existing laws, rules and regulations on
property and supply management to present fairly the book value of the
Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) in the financial statements by
performing the following corrective measures:

a. Direct the Inventory Committee to include the Construction in
Progress in the conduct of annual physical count of properties and
reflect the result of the physical count in the RPCPPE;
b. Require the City Engineer to provide the City Accountant and the
General Services Officer with complete description and cost
segregation of road components for road projects and other
infrastructure assets concerned to facilitate the preparation of
property records and PPE ledger cards;
c. Instruct the City Accountant and the GSO to maintain the
required records such as property cards and PPE ledger cards,
respectively, and provide the details of the PPE in the Report on
Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to
facilitate the conduct of physical count and reconciliation of
records; and
d. Take appropriate action against the concerned accountable
official/s for non-compliance and repeated unjustified failure to
comply with the requirements pursuant to Section 127 of PD 1445.

We recommended that the Honorable City Mayor instruct the Head
of the General Services Office to:

a. Cause the preparation of the updated Inventory and Inspection
Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) together with the
corresponding assessment, evaluation and appraisal report;
b. Facilitate the submission of request for disposal of the
aforementioned properties to the Office of the Audit Team for
inspection, evaluation and review by the COA Technical Services
Office; and
c. Ensure to undertake immediate disposal of the subject properties
upon receipt of the result of inspection from COA, so as to prevent
further deterioration, obsolescence and decline of value and in
order to generate additional funds from sale thereof.

We recommended that the Honorable City Mayor instruct the City
General Services Officer (GSO) to install markings “For Official Use Only”
with the official logo of the City, and to secure government plates
particularly those newly acquired motor vehicles of the LGU in compliance
with the provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61 to ensure the effective
conservation of energy and proper utilization of government motor vehicles.

We recommended that the Honorable City Mayor instruct the City
Treasurer to request from all depository banks to furnish the City
Accountant of complete copies of Credit Memo and Debit Memo every
month; and to direct the City Accountant to immediately record all valid
reconciling items duly supported with documents in the books of accounts in
compliance with the provisions of Paragraph 27 of IPSAS I, COA Circular
No. 96-011 to present fairly the balance of the Cash in Bank account in the
Financial Statements.

We further recommended that the City Treasurer and City
Accountant process the replacement of stale checks in accordance with the
provisions of Section 59, Volume I of Manual on the New Government
Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs.

We recommended that Management submit specific statutory
authority for the payment of financial assistance to Job Orders (JOs) and
Contract of Service (COS) workers of the LGU and various personnel of the
national government agency, non-governmental organizations and barangays
and the criteria used in selecting the beneficiaries pursuant to Section 4(1)
and 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 for further evaluation of the Audit Team;
otherwise, disbursements pertaining thereto shall be suspended or disallowed
in audit.

We further recommended that Management be more cautious in
granting financial assistance to ensure that the assistance were only provided
to the qualified recipients and to record its financial transactions consistent
with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units prescribed
by COA Circular 2015-009.

We recommended that Management:

a. Make a follow-up of the Decision from the Commission Proper (CP)
to determine whether the Petition for Review warrants a reversal to
the previous decision of the Regional Director; otherwise, the full
amount of the Ᵽ13,072,614.10 shall be disallowed in audit;
b. Ensure that all payments made against government funds are
supported with complete and valid documentation; and
c. Direct the city officials concerned to avoid the use of recoveries from
disallowances subject of Appeal or Petition for Review since the use of
the amount recovered is dependent on the outcome of the remedies
filed.

We recommended that:

a. City Accountant conduct a thorough analysis of the Due to NGAs
account and gather all relevant documents to determine the
appropriate actions that should be taken to facilitate the
reconciliation and elimination of dormant accounts;
b. City Accountant to reclassify the account inadvertently recorded
to Due to NGAs account into Due to GOCCs account to reflect the
correct balances of both accounts; and
c. City Treasurer to return to the respective source agencies the
unexpended balances of completed projects pursuant to COA
Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended that Management thru the initiative of the
Honorable City Mayor and enactment of Sangguniang Panlungsod
Resolution cause the transfer of the costs or book value of the implemented
buildings and other structures to the end users or recipient schools and the
barangays to conform with the provisions of paragraph 14 of IPSAS 17-
Property, Plant and Equipment. The transfer of properties should be
supported with complete documents to ensure proper recording in the books
of the LGU and of the recipient agencies.

We also recommended that the City Accountant provide the required
depreciation costs of the properties in the books of accounts pending the
transfer of ownership to the end users to present the actual valuation of the
properties in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that LGU Alaminos City make a follow-up with the PRA the
issuance of permit or approval from the President of the Philippines regarding
its application to undertake the Lucap Reclamation Project to prevent from
possible forfeiture in favor to the PRA or the Government pursuant to PD No.
1084, 3-A, as amended by EO No. 525 and EO No. 543.

We recommended that the Honorable City Mayor, through the City
Planning and Development Officer and the City Budget Officer, ensure that
all Projects/Programs/Activities (PPAs) charged against the 20%
Development Fund are in consonance with the provisions of Sections 2.0 and
3.0 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1 dated
February 22, 2017 and implemented within the target dates to attain the
intended purpose of the fund; and to require the City Engineer to provide
documents to support any delay in the implementation of projects; otherwise,
impose liquidated damages pursuant to Annex E, Item 8 of the 2016 Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act (RA) No. 9184.

We also recommended that Management carry-out the
aforementioned expenses in the General Fund and Special Education Fund
in accordance with its Appropriation Ordinance.

We recommended that Management ensure that Gender and
Development (GAD) Plan of the agency are implemented and will fully
address and adhere to the basic purpose and objectives towards the role of
women in becoming a participative partner in nation building and
development with the end view of protecting their lives and rights and to live
a modest life in a society.

We recommended that Management ensure compliance with Section
4, paragraph 2 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 by executing a Contract of
Lease/Agreement with the City Hundred Islands Employees Multi-Purpose
Cooperative (CHIEMPCO) which shall serve as the basis on the imposition
and collection of rental fees and utility bills to enable the LGU to generate
additional funds and also to protect the interest of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure compliance
with Section 322 of Republic Act 7160 and Section 5.1.11 of COA Circular
No. 2012-002 on the treatment of unexpended/unobligated balance of
LDRRMF for Capital Outlay by requiring the reversion of the Capital
Outlay in the amount of P6,981,436.70 back to the General Fund to ensure its
utilization to its intended purpose and to present fairly the balance of the
Trust Liabilities – DRRM account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict
compliance with the procurement provisions of the Revised IRR of Republic
Act No. 9184 and the fundamental principle on financial transactions
prescribed in Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and to:

a. Require the Bids and Awards Committee to submit explanation on
how the the prices quoted by the Suppliers in the four (4)
procurement of meals and snack were exactly the same with the
estimated unit costs of all the items in the Purchase Request (PR) of
the LGU; and
b. Instruct the BAC Secretariat and the City Accountant to submit
the lacking documents immediately to support the propriety,
legality and validity of the paid disbursements.

We recommended that the Honorable City Mayor require the
concerned Department Heads/Chiefs of Offices to:

a. properly plan, assess and evaluate the medical equipment/machine to
be procured in terms of availability of space, facilities and immediate
needs of the proposed Dialysis Centre to obtain maximum use of the
equipment in an economical, efficient and effective way and to avoid
wastage of government funds;
b. exhaust all possible means to ensure that utilization of the
hemodialysis machine and the like are maximized to prevent wear and
tear due to exposure to elements or through obsolescence or non-use;
and
c. submit explanation / justification on the immediate procurement of
the 5 units Hemodialysis Machine considering that the construction of
the Dialysis Centre Building is still on going at the time of purchase.

We also recommended that for future procurement of highly
specialized machines, management should ensure that the facilities to which
these machines shall be installed are ready for use.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
• draw Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to reclassify the PPE items totaling
P3,479,298.13 to its appropriate account; and
• drop the semi-expendable property by debiting the Accumulated Depreciation
and Government Equity accounts by P3,164,042.79 and P925,866.51,
respectively, and crediting the respective PPE accounts by P4,089,909.30.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
adjust the RPT/SET Receivables by debiting the RPT/SET Receivables and
crediting Deferred RPT/SET Income accounts by P20,570,091.02.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the Inventory Committee to submit the Report on the Physical Count of
the Local Road Network (RPCLRN);
b. instruct the General Services Officer (GSO) to maintain Local Road Network
Property Card (LRNPC) and prepare a Report on Local Road Network (RLRN);
c. request the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the
GSO the description and cost segregation of road components; and
d. direct the Municipal Accountant to keep and maintain Local Road Network
Ledger Card and reconcile the same with the results of physical inventory.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. ensure that the unexpended balance of Quick Release Fund (QRF) and
DRRMF- Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) are transferred
to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability-DRRM” in the
Trust Fund books; and
b. provide sufficient disclosure of the LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial
Statements as required under Section 5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to
disclose the adequate supporting information in the Notes to Financial Statements
in accordance with IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
prepare and maintain complete subsidiary records of each special account.
Accordingly, prepare trial balances and financial statements of each special
account at the end of each year in compliance with Section 110 of the MNGAS for
LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
deposit all collections intact and daily as required under Section 32 of MNGAS for
LGUs, Volume I.

Moreover, require the Municipal Treasurer to explain in writing the causes of delay
in the deposit of the collections of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer
to:
a. initiate the immediate disposal of the unserviceable property in accordance
with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and the rules and procedures provided in COA
Circular No. 92-386; and
b. submit the necessary documents, upon disposal, together with the Official
Receipt to the Accounting Office, as the basis of the latter to drop the
unserviceable property from the books of accounts in accordance with the
existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to
closely monitor the loans and insurance applications of the employees and
ensure that payroll deductions are in accordance with the order of preference
provided by RA No. 11260 and that the employees’ net take home pay shall not be
less than the minimum amount of P5,000.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Person to:
a. include in the submission of the GAD Accomplishment Report the documents
enumerated in Section 4.C.8.5 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint
Memorandum Circular 2013-01 to facilitate the evaluation of reported
accomplishments; and
b. submit the GAD Accomplishment Report within five working days from the end
of January of the preceding year in compliance with Item V of COA Circular No.
2014-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) to review and
update the Ten-Year Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan (2015-2024)
every two years thereafter or as the need arises in compliance with the
provisions of RA No. 9003;
b. establish a Solid Waste Management Board in accordance with the
requirements provided under the same RA to ensure effective and efficient
formulation and implementation of the Municipality’s Solid Waste Management
Plan.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has drawn JEV No. 2019-6-1018(100) dated June 3, 2019 for the reversion of the unexpended LDRRMF to the General Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to optimally utilize the LDRRMF and ensure that programs, projects and activities that could strengthen its disaster response capabilities and lessen the impacts of disasters are fully implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the projects be charged against the Special Education Fund. observations.managementAction: The Management exerted efforts to prioritize the full implementation of projects under the 20 per cent development fund. Three projects were delayed due to lack of planning and monitoring of projects.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Inventory
Committee exert effort to conduct complete annual physical count of all
properties of the City, and to prepare and submit the RPCPPE in accordance
with Section 124, Volume 1 of the MNGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that management dispose and drop the costs from
the books of all identified properties which are no longer in use in
accordance with Sections 76 and 79 of PD 1445 to:
1. Generate additional funds, when sold to be used for the agency’s
operations;
2. Relieve the responsible official of their accountability over said
properties;
3. Prevent said unserviceable properties from further deterioration
and loss of value; and
4. Allow the agency to use the space being occupied by these
unserviceable properties.

We recommended that the City GSO and the Inventory Committee
submit a duly accomplished Report on the Physical Count of Local road
Networks (RPCLRN) by supplying all the required information, duly signed
by all Inventory Committee and duly approved by the City Mayor. Likewise,
cause the reclassification of the recorded road networks worth P4,349,914.54
considered as repair and maintenance and not capital outlay. Lastly, include
in the Report of Physical Count of Local Road Network (RPCLRN) the road
networks amounting to P17,858,416.58 recorded under Trust Fund books to
complete the consolidated balance of P267,870,884.08 presented in the
Financial Statements in conformity with the provisions of PPSAS I.

We recommended further to require the Accounting Office to
immediately verify and adjust the deficiencies between the RPCLRN and the
Road Network Ledger Card (RNLC) in order to minimize, if not eliminate
the cause of the discrepancy.

We recommended that Management direct:
 The City Treasurer to require the petty cash custodian to stop
the utilization of the Petty Cash Fund for payment of
representation expenses; and
 All heads of offices to exercise prudence in the use of
government funds and see to it that their expenses for
representation and entertainment are limited only up to their
authorized representation allowances.

We recommended the City Mayor to require the designated Disbursing
Officer to adhere strictly with the provisions of Section 4(6) of P.D. 1445 to
ensure complete documentation before effecting payment of claims. Submit
the lacking documents provided in Section 10 of the Ordinance to preclude
the issuance of Notice of Suspension. (NS)

We recommended that Management refrain from accommodating
expenditures which do not fall under the allowable items to be charged
against the Petty Cash Fund. Ensure that government funds are spent in
accordance with the pertinent laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the City Accountant :
 Submit explanation/justification for the delay in the
recording/adjustment of identified current year reconciling
items reflected in the BRS.
 Submit explanation/justification for the delay of
recording/adjustment of prior year’s bank credits specifically
the unrecorded bank credits with total amount of ₱4,491.38.
Were these not detected/discovered during CY 2016 and CY
2017.
 Prepare a thorough verification of all outstanding reconciling
items not yet taken up in the books in order to present the
current amount of the Cash in Bank account balances as of
December 31, 2018 and thereafter furnish the Audit Team
copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) taking up the
adjustments.

We recommended that the concerned City officials and employees
submit the incomplete/deficient supporting documents for audit purposes
that may support the validity of disbursements and to preclude the issuance
of Notice of Suspension.

We recommended that the City Accountant to reclassify the
receivable accounts aged over 1 year to more than 10 years under non-
current assets, and effect correction/adjusting entries to fairly present the
account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that management intensify its review and
assessment of the receivable account and exert effort to collect from the
debtors. If collection is no longer possible, request for write-off of receivables
from the Commission on Audit with sufficient supporting documents and
justification in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005
dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the City Accountant prepare Journal Entry
Vouchers (JEVs) together with the supporting documents to adjust the
Inventory Accounts with balances per Accounting Records but already
inexistent per physical count. Require the requestors to submit
Acknowledgement Receipts in the distribution or issuance of the inventories
received by them.

We recommended that Management submit explanation/justification
on why the recorded loans payable remained unsettled for over seven (7)
years from the date of maturity.

We recommended further to provide the Audit Team copy of the
Loan Agreement and pertinent records to support the recorded amount.

We recommended that Management require the offices/department
involved to ensure the return of the unutilized balance of funds to the Source
Agency for projects that cannot possibly be undertaken pursuant to COA
Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended that Management :
a) Formulate a realistic and attainable work plan to ensure
subprojects and activities are ready for implementation upon
the release of the allocated funds; and install budget
performance tracking system to monitor the utilization of
funds; and
b) Fast track disbursement process in order to settle as soon as
possible the due and demandable obligations and prevent
accumulation of the unexpended fund balance.

We recommended that Management accomplish the targets it
committed for the year by ensuring the immediate implementation and
completion of on-going projects on time so that intended benefits will be
realized.

We recommended that Management submit the lacking/deficient
supporting documents for audit to support the validity/propriety of the time
extensions granted and to preclude the issuance of Notice of Suspensions as
applicable.

We recommended that the City officials and employees concerned
submit explanations/justification on the splitting of Purchase Orders (POs)
and payments.

Henceforth, strictly enforce and adhere to COA Circular No. 76-41,
and the provisions of R.A. 9184 to prevent or prohibit splitting in any form
or all forms for the protection and interest of the government.

We recommended City Tourism management to refrain from
involving any person in the collection function of the city if he/she is not a
regular bonded accountable officer and officially designated as revenue
collector.

We recommended that the City Development Council (CDC), City
Budget Officer and the City Planning and Development Coordinator
judiciously plan the programs/projects to be funded out of the 20%
Development Fund in accordance with the mandate of the Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 of the DILG and DBM. Ensure that the
20% Development Fund be limited to developmental projects and shall

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to:
a) Conduct thorough review, with the involvement of other accounting personnel, and analyze the details of receivables to ascertain their correctness, validity and collectability and exert extra effort which includes sending demand letters to persons who have known address to facilitate collections; and

Also, the Accounting Office should properly monitor entries in Due from Officers and Employees account to facilitate collections from the personnel before they separate from the service or be deducted from their terminal leave benefits to avoid long outstanding receivables.

The Accounting Office has designated one staff to work on the setting up and maintenance of Supplies Ledger Cards.
